nativeRange.habitat: The Endlicheri Bichir inhabits shallow, slow-moving or still waters — floodplains, swamps, marshy lake margins, and turbid river backwaters with soft substrates. These environments are typically low in dissolved oxygen, making access to the water surface for air-breathing essential to the fish's survival.

decorPreferences: fine sand substrate (essential — coarse gravel abrades the belly)
large smooth rocks and branchy driftwood arranged into caves
robust plants (Vallisneria, large Anubias, Aponogeton) or artificial cover
dim or diffused lighting to suit nocturnal habits
gentle to moderate filtration — strong turbulent flow is avoided in the wild
fully sealed lid with no gaps: foam or mesh over all cable/pipe openings — this species is a confirmed escape artist

diet.notes: A powerful piscivore in the wild; hunts by olfaction rather than vision. Offer large meaty foods: whole prawns, mussels, lancefish, earthworms, krill, chopped fish fillet, and large sinking carnivore pellets. Juveniles accept bloodworms and chunky frozen foods. Most specimens can be trained onto quality sinking pellets with patience. Feed after lights-out (2–3 times per week for adults); competition from faster-sighted tankmates at feeding time is a real risk — target feeding with tongs is advisable. Remove uneaten food promptly; this species produces heavy waste that spikes ammonia rapidly.

goodWith: other large Polypterus species of closely matched size in a 700 L+ tank
robust large African cichlids (Hemichromis bimaculatus, larger Tilapia-group)
large knifefish (Chitala, Notopterus) of comparable size
avoidWith: any fish small enough to fit in its mouth — will be eaten overnight
small or medium-sized Polypterus species (size mismatch leads to predation)
fin-nipping species (rasboras, tiger barbs, serpae tetras)
aggressive cichlids that target the soft belly and gill region
delicate or slow-moving fish unable to avoid the bichir's lunge

imageCandidates.notes: FishBase species image for Polypterus endlicherii; confirm current availability and license terms before use.

summary: The Endlicheri Bichir (Polypterus endlicherii), also known as the Saddled Bichir, is one of the largest bichirs available in the hobby, reaching 60–75 cm with bold dark saddle markings over a pale body. An obligate air-breather from the Nile, Lake Chad, and Congo basins, it demands a 500 L+ aquarium with a fully sealed lid and specialist-level care.
careNotes:
Polypterus endlicherii is among the largest bichirs in the hobby and belongs to the taxonomic ‘upper-jaw’ clade of Polypterus (species in which the upper jaw protrudes noticeably beyond the lower), alongside P. ornatipinnis. This distinguishes it from the ‘lower-jaw’ group (P. senegalus, P. delhezi, P. palmas), in which the lower jaw protrudes or jaws meet evenly. Native range spans the Nile basin, Lake Chad, the upper Congo, and connected West African river systems.

OBLIGATE AIR-BREATHER: Like all Polypteridae, P. endlicherii possesses a divided, lung-like swim bladder and is an obligate air-breather. It must have constant, unobstructed access to the water surface; any obstruction risks asphyxiation. Equally, it cannot be kept in a fully open-top tank — it will escape. The lid must be fully sealed with no gaps: all cable and pipe entry points should be blocked with filter foam or fine mesh. Bichirs can survive brief excursions on damp surfaces due to their air-breathing ability and have been found across rooms in the morning.

TANK REQUIREMENTS: A minimum of 500 L with a footprint of at least 200 cm in length is the practical minimum for a single adult; 700 L+ is strongly preferred. Fine sand is essential — coarse gravel or rough substrate will cause chronic abrasion ulcers on the belly. Decor should provide substantial daytime hiding (large driftwood caves, smooth rocks), as this species is strictly nocturnal and requires shelter to avoid chronic stress. Lighting should be dim or heavily diffused. Filtration must be powerful to handle heavy organic waste from the protein-rich diet, but turnover should be directed away from the main resting area to avoid strong currents.

FEEDING: P. endlicherii hunts by olfaction and has poor eyesight. In a community setting, faster-sighted tankmates will intercept food before the bichir locates it; target feeding with tongs or a feeding wand after lights-out is strongly recommended. Adults can be fed 2–3 times per week; remove uneaten food within an hour to prevent ammonia spikes, which this species is sensitive to. Most specimens will learn to accept large sinking carnivore pellets, which reduces the mess and disease risk associated with feeder fish; feeder fish should be avoided for biosecurity reasons.

COMPATIBILITY: Any tankmate that fits in the bichir’s considerable mouth gape will be consumed eventually. At 60–75 cm, that covers a very large proportion of hobby fish. Conspecific and mixed-Polypterus keeping is possible only in large tanks with closely size-matched individuals and multiple territorial anchors. Aggressive cichlids may attack the soft belly and gill region, causing serious wounds.

WATER QUALITY: Parameters should be stable rather than extreme. Weekly partial water changes of 30–40% are essential. Ammonia and nitrite must be maintained at zero; nitrates below 20 ppm. This species is copper-sensitive and cannot tolerate copper-based medications.

NOTE ON COMMON NAME: ‘Saddled Bichir’ refers to the pronounced dark saddle-like markings across the dorsal surface. The species is sometimes confused with P. ornatipinnis (Ornate Bichir) in the trade; P. endlicherii has larger, fewer, more diffuse saddle blotches compared to the bold cream-and-black banded pattern of P. ornatipinnis, and reliably reaches larger maximum sizes.
breedingNotes: Rarely bred in home aquaria; most specimens in the trade are wild-caught from West and Central African river systems. Sexual maturity is reached at approximately 5–7 years at substantial size. Spawning is triggered by rainy-season simulation: a period of slightly cooler, softer water followed by large water changes with warmer water, combined with high-protein conditioning feeds. During spawning the male wraps his anal fin around the female’s vent area and externally fertilises eggs as they are released; eggs are adhesive and scattered among fine-leafed plants or over substrate. Parents consume eggs if not removed promptly. Eggs hatch in approximately 3–4 days at 28°C; larvae bear prominent external gills that are gradually reabsorbed. Fry require live micro-foods (baby brine shrimp, micro-worms) initially. Juvenile bichirs must be size-sorted frequently to prevent predation among cohorts.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Polypterus endlicherii
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided adult size (to 75 cm), temperature 26–30°C, pH 6.5–7.8, hardness 5–20 dH, minimum tank recommendation, native range (Nile, Lake Chad, Niger, Congo), diet (piscivore/carnivore), obligate air-breather status, escape risk, nocturnal behaviour, copper sensitivity, compatible species, and breeding notes.

Confirmed ‘Saddled Bichir’ as recorded common name.

Noted as larger than Ornate Bichir in typical captive attainment; corroborated specialist-only advisory.

additional.notes: General bichir care guide used to cross-check tank size requirements, feeding approach (olfaction-based hunting, target feeding recommended), escape risk, fine-sand substrate requirement, and compatibility guidelines. Confirmed carnivore diet type and high escape risk across genus.

additional.name: TFH Magazine — The Big Bichirs: Polypterus endlicherii and relatives
additional.notes: Supplementary reference covering upper-jaw vs lower-jaw taxonomic distinction within Polypterus, P. endlicherii maximum size (60–75 cm), West/Central African range, piscivore feeding ecology, breeding trigger (rainy-season simulation), and comparison with P. ornatipinnis. Supported advanced care level and specialist-only advisory.

fish.breedingNotes: The Endlicheri Bichir is rarely bred in captivity, and most specimens in the hobby are wild-caught. Spawning is thought to be triggered by rainy-season simulation — a period of slightly cooler, softer water followed by large, warmer water changes and high-protein conditioning. The male wraps his anal fin around the female's vent to externally fertilise eggs as they are released; the adhesive eggs are scattered among plants or over substrate. Eggs hatch in approximately 3–4 days, and the larvae bear prominent external gills. Parents should be removed after spawning, as they will consume the eggs. Juveniles need to be size-sorted regularly to prevent predation among cohorts.

hobbyistAdvisory.reason: The Endlicheri Bichir reaches 60–75 cm and requires a minimum 500 L aquarium with a fully sealed, gapless lid, as it is an obligate air-breather and a confirmed escape artist. It is a powerful piscivore that will consume any tankmate small enough to fit in its mouth, and its long lifespan and large space requirements make it a commitment suited only to experienced specialist keepers.
